items.html:
Privacy is more than a closed door

When people think about privacy, they often picture a curtain drawn during personal care. That physical privacy matters, but the people you support also have a right to information privacy — control over who knows what about their lives, health and history. Every detail you learn on shift, from a diagnosis to a family worry, belongs to the person, not to you.

  • Confidentiality: keeping personal information private and sharing it only when there is a proper reason.
  • Data protection: the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 require records to be kept accurately, securely and no longer than needed.

Handling information the right way

Confidentiality is not secrecy. You share what you must to keep someone safe and well, and no more. A useful test is the need-to-know principle: does this colleague actually need this detail to do their job?

  • Ask before you share: seek consent where you can, and explain who will see the information.
  • Keep records safe: lock away paper notes, log out of care systems, and never discuss people in lifts, corridors or on social media.
  • Know the limits: if someone is at risk of harm, you may need to pass information on to keep them safe, and you should tell your manager.

items.question: A relative you have never met phones the office and asks how their father slept last night. What is the safest response?

answers.text: Explain you cannot share information without knowing they are entitled to it, and check with your manager first.
answers.feedback: Correct. Verifying identity and the person's own wishes about sharing protects their confidentiality.

answers.text: Give a brief update, since it is only a family member asking.
answers.feedback: Not safe. You cannot confirm the caller's identity or whether the person wants information shared with them.

answers.text: Refuse all contact and hang up without explanation.
answers.feedback: That goes too far. You can stay polite and helpful while still protecting confidentiality.

answers.text: Ask another resident who knows the family whether it is fine to share.
answers.feedback: No. Discussing someone's care with an unrelated person is itself a breach of confidentiality.

items.html:
Every person is an individual

Dignity means seeing the whole person, not a task or a diagnosis. The people you support have their own history, beliefs, culture, language and relationships that make them who they are. Treating everyone the same is not the same as treating everyone fairly — real respect means responding to what matters to this person.

  • Protected characteristics: the Equality Act 2010 protects things such as religion, race, sex, sexual orientation, age and disability from discrimination.
  • Identity: a person's faith, culture and preferred name or pronouns are part of who they are, and upholding them is part of dignity.

Respecting preferences in daily care

Small choices carry a lot of meaning. The way someone likes to be addressed, the food they can and cannot eat, how they dress or pray, and whether they prefer a carer of a particular gender all deserve respect.

  • Ask, don't assume: two people of the same religion may practise it very differently, so find out what each person wants.
  • Record preferences: note them in the care plan so the whole team follows them consistently.
  • Support, don't judge: a person's choices about relationships, culture or lifestyle are theirs to make.

items.question: Because two people you support share the same religion, it is efficient to assume they observe the same dietary and worship customs.
items.correctMsg: Correct — that statement is false. People practise faith and culture in their own individual ways, so you should ask each person about their preferences.
items.wrongMsg: Not right. Assuming everyone with the same faith is identical ignores individuality; always check each person's own preferences.

items.html:
Care done with people, not to them

Active participation means recognising each person as an active partner in their own care and daily life, rather than a passive recipient of it. When you support someone to do what they can for themselves, you protect their independence, skills and sense of worth.

  • Do with, not for: let someone wash the parts of their body they can reach, or choose and pass their own clothes, even if it takes longer.
  • Wellbeing: taking part builds confidence and can reduce the frustration and low mood that come with feeling helpless.

Involving people in their own care

People have the right to be involved in decisions about their support — a principle often summed up as ‘nothing about me without me’. Standard 7 asks you to actively encourage people to make choices and to help them take part in planning their care.

  • Offer real choices: what to wear, when to get up, what to eat, how to spend the day.
  • Share the care plan: involve the person and, where they wish, the people close to them in reviewing it.
  • Listen to feedback: act on what people tell you, and pass concerns you cannot resolve to your manager.

items.question: Mr Okafor can wash his own face and hands but finds it slow, and you are running behind. What best supports active participation?

answers.text: Give him the flannel and the time to wash what he can, and help only with what he cannot manage.
answers.feedback: Correct. Doing with rather than for maintains his independence and dignity.

answers.text: Wash him quickly yourself so the round stays on time.
answers.feedback: This removes his independence and treats the schedule as more important than the person.

answers.text: Tell him he must be quicker tomorrow or you will have to do it for him.
answers.feedback: This pressures him and undermines his confidence and choice.

answers.text: Skip washing his face today to save time.
answers.feedback: This neglects his care and his right to be clean and comfortable.

items.html:
Privacy where space is shared

In residential and supported living settings, people still have a right to privacy even when space is shared. A bedroom or bathroom is someone's private space, and simple habits show respect for it.

  • Knock and wait: always knock, announce yourself and wait for an answer before entering.
  • Use screens and curtains: protect people from view during personal care, even in a shared bay.
  • Respect belongings and post: do not open someone's mail, read their phone or move their things without permission.
  • Private conversations: help people make calls or see visitors somewhere they cannot be overheard.

Technology and monitoring

Door cameras, movement sensors, acoustic monitoring and CCTV can help keep people safe, but they can also intrude on privacy. Monitoring should only be used when it is genuinely in the person's interest and is the least restrictive way to meet a need.

  • Consent and capacity: where a person can decide, their agreement is needed; where they cannot, a best-interests decision must be made and recorded.
  • Proportionate: a sensor that alerts staff to a fall may intrude far less than a camera watching a bedroom.
  • Be open: people and their families should know what is monitored and why.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete the guidance on privacy and monitoring.
items.textField: Before entering a bedroom you should *knock* and wait for an answer. Monitoring such as a camera should be the least *restrictive* option and used only when it is *proportionate* to the need. Where a person cannot decide for themselves, a *best-interests* decision must be recorded, and families should be told what is *monitored* and why.

items.html:
Putting privacy and dignity together

Privacy and dignity are not extra tasks — they run through everything you do. Think about a typical morning: how you greet someone, whether you knock, how you protect their information, and whether you offer real choices. Each moment is a chance to uphold or undermine dignity.

  • Scenario: a person declines a shower and wants to stay in yesterday's clothes. Respecting that choice, while gently offering options, upholds their dignity and autonomy.
  • Scenario: a colleague talks loudly about a resident's continence in the lounge. Speaking up protects that person's confidentiality and self-respect.

Dignity day to day — a quick recap

You can protect privacy and dignity through everyday habits that soon become second nature.

  • Respect the person: use their preferred name, listen, and treat them as an individual.
  • Protect privacy: knock, cover, and guard personal information.
  • Offer choice: support people to decide and to do what they can for themselves.
  • Speak up: challenge poor practice and report concerns so people are kept safe and treated with respect.

items.question: A person you support wants to wear an outfit that another carer thinks looks odd. What upholds their dignity?

answers.text: Support their choice of clothing, as how they dress is part of their identity and autonomy.
answers.feedback: Correct. Respecting personal choices, including dress, is central to dignity and individuality.

answers.text: Choose different clothes for them to avoid comments from others.
answers.feedback: This overrides the person's choice and treats others' opinions as more important than their wishes.

answers.text: Let them wear it but tell them it looks odd.
answers.feedback: This is disrespectful and undermines their confidence and dignity.

answers.text: Ask other people present to vote on the outfit.
answers.feedback: This humiliates the person; their clothing is their own decision, not a group matter.
